abstract: On June 16, 2026, the European Parliament voted to approve changes to the EU AI Act's compliance timeline. Many readers now assume the high risk deadline has settled at December 2027. It has not, not yet. This article explains what the EU AI Act requires, why the high risk category matters, what the June 2026 vote did and did not accomplish, and what organizations in Austria and the wider European Union should do while the legal process finishes. It draws on primary sources from the European Parliament, the Council of the European Union, and the Official Journal, alongside legal analysis from international law firms tracking the file in real time.
body:
\## Introduction



The EU AI Act, formally Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, is the world's first comprehensive law governing artificial intelligence across all sectors. The Act was designed to take effect in stages, with different obligations becoming applicable over several years.

A key compliance milestone for High-Risk AI systems was scheduled for August 2, 2026. However, in June 2026, the European Parliament approved a proposal to postpone that deadline by sixteen months. This triggered widespread confusion, with many reports presenting the extension as already in force. In reality, parliamentary approval alone does not amend EU law, and the legal status of the original deadline remains an important compliance question. This article examines two issues: (i) whether the August 2, 2026 deadline remains legally applicable and (ii) what organizations developing or deploying AI systems within the European Union should do while the legislative process remains ongoing.



\## The EU AI Act's Risk Pyramid



The EU AI Act does not regulate all AI systems the same way. It sorts them into four tiers based on potential for harm, and the obligations a system faces depend entirely on which tier it falls into.



\[[figure-1]]



At the top sits unacceptable risk: practices the Act bans outright, such as social scoring by public authorities and manipulative AI that exploits a person's vulnerabilities. There is no compliance pathway here, only the choice not to build the system.



Below that sits high risk, the category this article concerns. These systems are not banned, but face the Act's heaviest obligations: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, human oversight, and logging. Annex III lists the use cases involved: employment decisions such as hiring or performance evaluation, biometric identification, access to education, creditworthiness assessment, and certain uses in law enforcement and border management. If your organization uses AI to screen job applicants, score loan applications, or evaluate students, this tier almost certainly applies to you.



Limited risk sits below that, where the main obligation is transparency, a chatbot must generally disclose that the user is talking to AI rather than a person. At the bottom, the largest tier by far, sits minimal risk: spam filters, AI powered game opponents, and recommendation engines, with no special obligations at all. This article concerns the high risk tier specifically, since that is the tier whose timeline just became a matter of public confusion.



\## The Gap Between Legal Obligations and Implementation Readiness

The next major milestone under the EU AI Act was scheduled for August 2, 2026. From that date, organizations developing High-Risk AI systems would be required to comply with the Act's core obligations, including risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record keeping, transparency, human oversight, and requirements relating to accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. Organizations deploying such systems would also become subject to a range of operational obligations. These requirements represent some of the most significant compliance provisions within the Act and carry substantial financial consequences. For the most serious violations, penalties can reach €35 million or 7 percent of an organization's global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

As preparations for implementation progressed, concerns emerged that key parts of the compliance framework would not be ready in time. Several technical standards intended to support implementation remained under development, including guidance relating to documentation, risk management processes, human oversight measures, audit records, and system testing. At the same time, some Member States had not yet completed the regulatory and enforcement structures required to supervise compliance. The concern was not whether the obligations should exist, but whether organizations and regulators had sufficient practical guidance and infrastructure to implement them effectively.

In response, the European Commission proposed postponing the High-Risk AI compliance deadlines. Under the proposal, obligations for standalone High-Risk AI systems would move from August 2, 2026 to December 2, 2027, while obligations for High-Risk AI systems incorporated into regulated products, such as medical devices and machinery, would move from August 2, 2027 to August 2, 2028. The proposal also introduces a prohibition on AI systems that generate fake intimate images of individuals without their consent.

The proposal subsequently received political support within the EU legislative process and was approved by the European Parliament on June 16, 2026. However, parliamentary approval alone does not amend EU law. Until the remaining legislative steps are completed and the amendment is formally adopted and published, the original compliance deadlines established by the EU AI Act remain legally applicable.


\[[figure-2]]




\## Political Support Does Not Equal Legal Certainty

The proposed extension has received political support and approval from the European Parliament, but it has not yet become law. Formal adoption and publication are still required before the revised dates take legal effect. Until that occurs, the original August 2, 2026 deadline remains legally applicable. For compliance purposes, organizations should distinguish between an expected outcome and a legally effective one.

The proposed extension may ultimately become law, but that does not eliminate the need for continued preparation. The technical standards and implementation guidance required to support compliance remain under development, meaning that organizations that pause governance and compliance efforts today may gain little practical advantage even if the deadline is extended.


\## What to Do Now


For that reason, organizations should continue building their AI governance, risk management, documentation, and compliance processes on the assumption that the current legal deadline remains in force. If the extension is formally adopted, they gain additional implementation time. If it is not, they avoid the operational and regulatory risks associated with last-minute compliance efforts. This reflects a broader principle of regulatory risk management: compliance programs should be built around legally effective obligations, not anticipated legislative outcomes.



\## Conclusion


While the proposed extension of the EU AI Act's High-Risk AI deadlines has received significant political support, it has not yet completed the legislative process required to give it legal effect. As a result, the original August 2, 2026 deadline remains the legally applicable position at the time of writing.

Organizations should therefore continue preparing on the basis of the current law rather than an anticipated outcome. In regulatory compliance, legal obligations are determined by what has been formally adopted, not by what is widely expected to happen.
citation: Mpofu, E. (2026). The EU AI Act High Risk Deadline: What the June 2026 Vote Changes and What It Does Not.
author: Dr. Ephraim Mpofu

subtitle: What the June 2026 Parliament vote really changed, what still remains legally unresolved, and what organisations should do now.
title: The EU AI Act High Risk Deadline: What the June 2026 Vote Changes and What It Does Not
